talk_title: One Gene at a Time: Metabolic Adaptations Driving Metastatic Growth in Pancreatic Cancer
MOA_summary: The lab identified metastasis‑specific metabolic genes (GSTT1, SLC30A4, MAT2A) that are up‑regulated in PDAC liver and lung metastases and are required and sufficient for metastatic colonization; GSTT1 drives extracellular matrix remodeling via fibronectin glutathionylation, SLC30A4 mediates serine uptake addiction, and MAT2A translocates to the nucleus to produce SAM for chromatin methylation in metastatic cells.
key_findings: GSTT1 is absent in primary PDAC but highly expressed in liver and lung metastases and its knockout abolishes metastatic outgrowth while primary tumor growth is unaffected
GSTT1 overexpression induces ectopic metastases in multiple organs and promotes fibronectin secretion via glutathione conjugation

SLC30A4 is specifically up‑regulated in metastatic lesions, and its loss impairs 3D colony formation and metastatic colonization; overexpression increases serine uptake and rescues growth in serine‑depleted conditions
MAT2A shifts from cytoplasmic to nuclear localization in metastatic cells, supporting nuclear SAM production for histone methylation; nuclear MAT2A is required for metastatic growth
open_questions: What transcription factors drive the coordinated up‑regulation of GSTT1, SLC30A4 and MAT2A in disseminated tumor cells?
Can pharmacologic inhibition of GSTT1 or SLC30A4 antibodies prevent metastatic colonization in vivo?

talk_title: Targeting IMPDH2 and Ferroptosis to Suppress Metastatic Pancreatic Cancer

speaker.name: Dr. Pankaj Singh
speaker.affiliation: University of Oklahoma Health Sciences Center

models.name: PDAC metastasis mouse splenic injection model

MOA_summary: IMPDH2 is overexpressed in PDAC metastases and drives purine biosynthesis; chronic mycophenolic acid (MPA) exposure induces resistance via up‑regulation of glutathione metabolism (SLC7A11, GPX4); co‑targeting ferroptosis (RSL3) or cystine uptake (sulfasalazine) restores MPA sensitivity and markedly reduces metastatic burden in mouse models.
key_findings: IMPDH2 expression is high in primary and metastatic PDAC and correlates with poor patient survival
Long‑term MPA exposure selects for PDAC cells with increased glutathione metabolism (up‑regulation of SLC7A11, GPX4) and a 20‑fold increase in IC50
Sulfasalazine sensitizes MPA‑resistant cells to MPA in vitro and modestly reduces tumor burden in vivo
Combination of MPA with the ferroptosis inducer RSL3 enhances cell killing and decreases liver metastasis size in mouse splenic injection models
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Acquired MPA resistance is mediated by up‑regulation of cystine‑glutathione axis, conferring cross‑resistance to oxidative stress

combination_strategies.rationale: Inhibit cystine uptake to deplete glutathione and overcome MPA resistance
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced sensitivity of resistant PDAC cells to MPA; modest tumor reduction in vivo

combination_strategies.rationale: Induce ferroptosis by inhibiting GPX4, synergizing with purine synthesis inhibition
combination_strategies.phenotype: greater cell death across MPA‑sensitive and resistant lines; reduced metastatic nodules in mice

open_questions: Will combining MPA with KRAS inhibitors improve efficacy in metastatic PDAC?
Can dietary serine or methionine restriction augment the anti‑metastatic effect of IMPDH2 inhibition?

talk_title: NUDT5‑PPAT Interaction Controls Purine Biosynthesis Switch and Thiopurine Sensitivity

clinical_data.comparator: NUDT5 KO or PPAT‑binding mutant xenografts

MOA_summary: NUDT5 binds PPAT via its R70 residue, promoting PPAT tetramerization and suppressing de novo purine synthesis; loss of NUDT5 or disruption of the NUDT5‑PPAT interaction maintains PPAT in an active dimeric state, rendering cells resistant to thiopurines and de novo pathway inhibitors.
key_findings: NUDT5 deficiency confers resistance to 6‑thioguanine and to de novo pathway inhibitors (methotrexate, leflunomide)
NUDT5 directly interacts with PPAT; the R70A mutation abolishes binding without affecting catalytic activity
Wild‑type NUDT5 (or catalytic dead) induces PPAT tetramerization and reduces its enzymatic activity by ~80% in vitro
In xenografts, tumors expressing NUDT5‑binding‑defective PPAT remain resistant to 6‑TG, whereas WT NUDT5 restores drug sensitivity
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: NUDT5‑mediated PPAT tetramerization provides a novel mechanism of metabolic drug resistance
open_questions: What cellular signal triggers NUDT5‑PPAT binding during purine abundance?
Does NUDT5 regulate purinosome dynamics in vivo and can this be therapeutically exploited?
